# E2e Key Exchange

> Manages cryptographic key material for end-to-end encrypted messaging. Handles device key publication, one-time pre-key upload/claiming, and cross-server key queries.

**Category:** Auth · **Version:** 1.0.0 · **Tags:** e2e · encryption · key-exchange · olm · megolm · devices · security

## What this does

Manages cryptographic key material for end-to-end encrypted messaging. Handles device key publication, one-time pre-key upload/claiming, and cross-server key queries.

Specifies 8 acceptance outcomes that any implementation must satisfy, regardless of language or framework.

## Fields

- **device_id** *(token, required)* — Identifier of the device the keys belong to
- **user_id** *(text, required)* — Account the device belongs to
- **identity_keys** *(json, required)* — Long-lived public keys for the device (curve25519 and ed25519)
- **one_time_keys** *(json, optional)* — Single-use pre-keys uploaded in bulk to bootstrap Olm sessions
- **fallback_keys** *(json, optional)* — Fallback pre-keys used when no one-time keys remain
- **claimed_keys** *(json, optional)* — One-time keys retrieved by another device to initiate encryption
- **failures** *(json, optional)* — Map of server domains to error descriptions for failed queries

## What must be true

- **key_management:** The server stores device keys and one-time keys but never stores private key material, One-time keys are consumed exactly once; after claiming they are removed from the pool, When one-time keys are exhausted, fallback keys are used and marked for replacement, Devices that are deleted have their associated keys removed from the store
- **querying:** Device key queries must be separated by domain: local devices from database, remote via federation, Remote device key results are cached to reduce federation load; stale cache entries refreshed on demand, Servers that are unreachable during a query are recorded in the failures response, Servers previously marked as down are not contacted until their backoff window expires

## Success & failure scenarios

**✅ Success paths**

- **Device Keys Uploaded** — when device is authenticated; uploaded keys include valid signatures from the device, then Device keys are discoverable by other users in shared rooms.
- **One Time Keys Uploaded** — when device is authenticated; uploaded one-time keys have valid signatures, then Other devices can claim these keys to establish Olm sessions.
- **One Time Key Claimed** — when requester specifies one or more target devices; target device has at least one unclaimed one-time key, then Requester can use the claimed key to establish an encrypted Olm session.
- **Fallback Key Used** — when target device has no unclaimed one-time keys; a fallback key is available, then Encryption session established using fallback key; device should upload new one-time keys.
- **Keys Queried Local** — when queried users are all on the local server, then Device keys returned from local database with cross-signing bundles.
- **Keys Queried Federated** — when queried users include users on remote servers; remote servers are reachable, then Keys for remote users returned; unreachable servers listed in failures.
- **Key Query Partial Failure** — when one or more remote servers are unreachable or in backoff, then Available keys returned; caller can retry for failed servers later.

**❌ Failure paths**

- **No Keys Available** — when target device has no one-time keys and no fallback key, then No key returned for that device; encryption session cannot be established. *(error: `KEY_EXCHANGE_NO_KEYS`)*

## Errors it can return

- `KEY_EXCHANGE_NO_KEYS` — No one-time keys available for this device
- `KEY_EXCHANGE_QUERY_FAILED` — Key query could not be completed for one or more servers

## Connects to

- **cross-signing-verification** *(recommended)* — Cross-signing keys are bundled in key query responses alongside device keys
- **device-management** *(required)* — Device keys are invalidated when the device is deleted
- **key-backup-recovery** *(recommended)* — Session keys established during key exchange are backed up for recovery
- **server-federation** *(recommended)* — Remote device keys are fetched via federation queries
